meta.title: Introduction to Squeeze Play
meta.description: The most elegant technique in bridge

blocks.content:
## The Most Beautiful Play in Bridge

You've counted your winners. You're one short of your contract. There's no finesse to take, no long suit to establish, no ruff available. It looks hopeless.

But then you notice something: one defender is guarding two different suits. If you run your winners in a third suit, that defender will eventually have to discard from one of the two suits they're guarding. When they do, your "hopeless" card in that suit becomes a winner.

This is a **squeeze** — and it's the most elegant technique in all of card play.

## How a Squeeze Works

A squeeze forces a defender to make an impossible choice. They're guarding two suits, but they only have room for cards in one. When you play your winners, they must give up one guard — and you win the trick they abandoned.

### A Simple Example

It's the last 4 tricks. You need all 4. You're in your hand:

```
Dummy:  ♠ —
        ♥ A 3
        ♦ —
        ♣ 7

You:    ♠ —
        ♥ 5
        ♦ A
        ♣ 4

West:   ♠ —
        ♥ K Q
        ♦ —
        ♣ Q

East:   ♠ J
        ♥ —
        ♦ 8
        ♣ J
```

You play the ♦A (your "squeeze card"). East discards harmlessly. But look at **West's** problem:

- West has ♥KQ guarding against dummy's ♥A3
- West has ♣Q guarding against your ♣4 (and dummy's ♣7)
- West must discard one card

If West throws a heart (say the ♥Q), dummy's ♥A and ♥3 are both winners. If West throws the ♣Q, your ♣4 (or dummy's ♣7) becomes a winner.

West is **squeezed** — there's no winning discard.

## The BLUE Requirements

For a simple squeeze to work, you need four things (remembered by the mnemonic **BLUE**):

### B — Busy Defender
One defender must be the sole guard of two suits. If both defenders can guard a suit, there's no squeeze — one can let go while the other holds.

### L — Loser(s)
You must have exactly the right number of losers — typically one more winner than you need, minus one. In practice, this means you're exactly one trick short. (If you have all the tricks you need, you don't need a squeeze. If you're two short, a simple squeeze can't help.)

More precisely: you should have all but one of the remaining tricks as winners, with one "loser" that the squeeze will convert into a winner.

### U — Upper Threat
You need a threat card (a card that will become a winner if the defender unguards that suit) in the **same hand as the squeeze card** or accessible via an entry.

### E — Entry
You need an entry to the hand with the established threat. After the squeeze card forces a discard, you must be able to reach the winner it created.

## Walking Through a Full Squeeze

You're declaring 3NT. You have 8 winners and need 9. After the opening lead, the position near the end is:

```
         ♠ 7
         ♥ A 5
         ♦ —
         ♣ —

♠ —              ♠ —
♥ K J            ♥ T
♦ —              ♦ 9
♣ —              ♣ —

         ♠ —
         ♥ 6
         ♦ J
         ♣ —
```

You (South) need all 3 remaining tricks. You have the ♦J (a winner), ♥6 (looks useless), and dummy has ♠7 (looks useless), ♥A5.

Play the ♦J (squeeze card). East discards the ♦9 (irrelevant). West must keep ♥KJ to guard against the ♥A5. But West only has 2 cards! If West discards... wait, West has exactly ♥KJ and must discard one.

Actually, let's look at this more carefully. After the ♦J is played, West must reduce to 1 card. If West keeps the ♥K, dummy's ♥5 is good (cash ♥A, ♥5 wins). If West keeps the ♥J, dummy's ♥A wins and ♥5... well, West still has one heart.

Let me show a cleaner example:

```
         ♠ A
         ♥ 7
         ♦ —
         ♣ —

♠ K              ♠ —
♥ Q              ♥ —
♦ —              ♦ T
♣ —              ♣ 9

         ♠ 3
         ♥ —
         ♦ —
         ♣ A
```

You need 2 tricks from the last 2 cards in each hand. You play the ♣A (squeeze card). East discards harmlessly.

West is squeezed:
- If West discards the ♠K → dummy's ♠A wins, then ♥7 is irrelevant, but your ♠3 is now a winner
- If West discards the ♥Q → dummy's ♥7 becomes a potential winner... wait.

Actually the simplest illustration:

```
         ♠ A 3
         ♥ —
         ♦ —
         ♣ —

♠ K Q            ♠ —
♥ —              ♥ —
♦ —              ♦ J
♣ —              ♣ 9

         ♠ —
         ♥ —
         ♦ —
         ♣ A 2
```

You play the ♣A. East discards a diamond. West must discard from ♠KQ. Whether West throws the K or Q, dummy's ♠A3 takes both tricks. Before the squeeze, you had 1 winner (♣A) and needed 2 more. After West is forced to unguard spades, you get both.

## Recognizing Squeeze Potential

You don't need to see the full ending to suspect a squeeze. Look for these clues:

1. **You're exactly one trick short** — squeezes gain exactly one trick
2. **One defender seems to be guarding two suits** — based on the bidding and play
3. **You have threat cards** in the defended suits — cards that will win if the defender lets go
4. **You can run a long suit** — the squeeze card is the last winner in a suit you're cashing

When these conditions are met, simply **run your winners** and watch what happens. Even if you can't see the full position, the defender might be squeezed without you realizing it in advance. This is called **playing for the squeeze" — it costs nothing to try.

## Squeezes in Practice

Squeezes are relatively rare at the table — perhaps 1-2 per session. But they're disproportionately satisfying because they create tricks from apparently nothing. And the beauty of a squeeze is that you often don't need to do anything special — just cash your winners and let the defender suffer.

blocks.title: Finding the Squeeze
blocks.solution:
You're declaring 6NT. You have 11 winners and need 12. The last 5 cards:

```
         ♠ A 7
         ♥ —
         ♦ 5
         ♣ —
         ♣ —
```

Wait, let me set up a clean position.

```
Dummy:   ♠ A 5
         ♥ 7
         ♦ —
         ♣ —

You:     ♠ 3
         ♥ —
         ♦ A
         ♣ 4
```

You know from the play that West holds ♠KQ and ♥Q (West bid hearts and played spade honors earlier). West has 3 cards left and must keep 2 spades to guard dummy's ♠A5, and must keep the ♥Q to guard dummy's ♥7.

But West only has room for 3 cards — and needs 3 guards (♠K, ♠Q, ♥Q). Right now that works. But when you play the ♦A (your squeeze card), West must reduce to 2 cards.

If West throws a spade: dummy's ♠A5 are both winners (♠A, then ♠5 after the remaining spade honor is the only one left).

If West throws the ♥Q: dummy's ♥7 is promoted to a winner.

Either way, you make your 12th trick. The squeeze works because you had all the BLUE requirements: Busy defender (West guards both suits), Loser (you were one short), Upper threat (♥7 in dummy), and Entry (♠A to reach the established winner).

blocks.content: A squeeze forces a defender who guards two suits to give up one guard when you play your winners. You don't always need to see the full ending — just run your winners when you're one trick short and a defender seems to be guarding two suits. Squeezes are rare but deeply satisfying, and they reward precise play throughout the hand.

reviewQuestions.question: What does BLUE stand for in squeeze play requirements?
options: Busy defender, Loser, Upper threat, Entry
reviewQuestions.explanation: BLUE: Busy defender (one player guards two suits), Loser (you're exactly one trick short), Upper threat (a card that wins if they unguard), Entry (to reach the established trick).
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: You've got the mnemonic! These four conditions are what make a simple squeeze possible.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: BLUE = Busy defender, Loser, Upper threat, Entry. These are the four requirements for a simple squeeze to work.

reviewQuestions.question: How many extra tricks does a simple squeeze typically gain?
reviewQuestions.explanation: A simple squeeze gains exactly one trick — it converts your 'one trick short' position into exactly enough. For more tricks, you'd need a double squeeze or repeated squeezes.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Right! One trick from nothing — that's the magic of the squeeze. It turns an impossible position into a winner.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: A simple squeeze gains exactly one trick. You start one short and the squeeze creates the extra trick you need.
